comment_content:
This course goes over the basics and theory of cloud computing and big data systems (as titled). Personally, it wasn't particularly rewarding. Its high enrollment is probably due to it being a BDT minor compulsory course. I believe the syllabus this semester, coordinated by GUO Song, is slightly different to previous offerings. Below is an over-simplification and heavily-opinionated list of topics introduced in this course. (I am not responsible for certain inaccuracies)

Topic 1: Overview & Course Logistics
- There are a lot of data!
- Course Logistics (duh)
- How to read a paper (He wanted to introduce this to us)

Topic 2: Fundamentals
- What is Cloud? (Cloud big!)
- Cloud definitions and characteristics (Cloud big!!)
- Amazon EC2 (Our sponsor)
- Why Cloud? Cloud cheap! (In certain contexts)

Topic 3: Service Models and Challenges
- Cloud deployment models (Public or private? Or maybe both?)
- Cloud service models (Something-aaS)
- Issues of Cloud
- Challenges Facing Cloud Providers

Topic 4: Basic Data Analytics (A lot of COMP 2211 content)
- Basic Concepts (Take COMP 2211)
- Predictive Analytics (Take COMP 2211 and/or COMP 4211)
- Descriptive Analytics (Take COMP 2211)

(btw I think GUO Song is teaching COMP 2211 in Spring 2024-25)

Topic 5: Cloud Storage Systems
- How does Cloud store data? What are problems faced?
- Details about Cloud Storage Systems

Topic 6: Hadoop Distributed File System (HDFS)
- What is HDFS?
- Design
- Limitations
- uh oh there is Java code

Topic 7: MapReduce
- Parallel processing is faster and better (probably)
- Functional Programming (COMP 3031!) (No seriously PPL saved my ass)
- Map (duh)
- Reduce (duh)

Topic 8: MapReduce Algorithm Design
- Zen of MapReduce (idfk)
- PseudoCode! (Important for exam)
- Oh there's also the Combiner aside from Mapper and Reducer
- Time to be mean (MapReduce to calculate mean)
- Bigram Counting - Pairs and Stripes (uh... COMP 4221 I guess)

-- Midterm syllabus ends here --

Topic 9: Hadoop
- We're back! (Kind of)
- Hadoop is better than MapReduce from scratch
- Get a job (Hadoop job)
- MapReduce in Hadoop

Topic 10: Virtualization
- What is virtualization? (COMP 3511)
- History
- How does it work?
- Implementations
- VM & Containers

Topic 11: From MapReduce to Spark
- Hadoop is great, except not really
- Apache Spark is great! (Why Java when you can Python?)
- Map and Reduce in Spark (Functional Programming but easier)

Topic 12: Spark Internals
- RDD
- Spark Architecture
- YARN
- Shuffling
- Spark vs Hadoop MapReduce

Topic 13: Spark Programming
- PySpark API
- RDD with Python (e.g. rdd.map(...).map(...).reduce(...))

Topic 14: From RDDs to DataFrames
- DataFrames means less code (probably)
- Spark SQL
- More Python programing!

Topic 15: Graph Analytics
- Graph (COMP 2711/COMP 3711)
- PageRank (COMP 4321)
- Doing these in Spark
- Dijkstra's Algorithm (I know, I also thought 'wtf')
- Parallel BFS (fun) (not)
- Application

-- Final syllabus are everything above, with a focus on the latter half --

Overall, if you aren't taking BDT, I wouldn't particularly recommend this course. It is, however, a decent course if you want a low-ish workload 4000 COMP course and you don't really care much about learning.
comment_teaching:
GUO Song was a decent professor. He clearly has the heart in teaching, but unfortunately his lectures are Mon 1330-1450 and Fri 0900-1020, which means people don't show up to his Friday lectures much. He talks at a moderate pace in my opinion with a clear accent. He does articulate his b, p, t and ks louder than other consonants. He also often draws demonstrations of concepts on the whiteboard, which is beneficial for understanding. He seems to be happy to answer any questions from students, and also encourage people to make appointments face-to-face with him instead of using emailing as the may way of communication after-class. Given the content of this course, I believe the delivery was pretty good.

I would, however, say that the TAs of this course aren't particularly good. They make announcements later than I would like them, whether it's exam details or HW logistics. This course uses GitHub classroom for HW and project submissions, and I would really appreciate if they actually updated details instead of copying them from previous semesters.

For HW2, the TA half-screwed up the autograding, where a lot of students encountered a timeout and got 0 points. After the appeal period, however, the statistics of the HW increased significantly. I would've appreciated it a lot more if it didn't give me a heart attack over initially receiving a 0 on my HW2.

C mostly because of TAs. GUO Song himself is at B.
comment_workload:
Workload is low for a 4000-level course.
The HWs are annoying but they can be finished within half a day usually. Plus, if you know where to look, sources are available.
The workload of the project wasn't particularly high, but it heavily depends on your topic.

B because it isn't low per se, but it isn't particularly energy-draining.
comment_grading:
Statistics below are given in the format of: Mean/High/Upper Quartile/Median/Lower Quartile/Low
The grading scheme:

1. 10% In-class Quizzes and Participation
No details were particularly announced beforehand, but GUO Song created 3 in-class quizzes. They were in the format of the exams (except shorter). These fully open-book quizzes (except the first one) are essentially attendance checks. You get 1 if you submitted, 0 otherwise. They act as a guide to prepare you for what the midterm and final exams will be like.

Statistics are not completely disclosed, but:
Quiz 1 (Full: 1 point)
0.96/1/1/1/1/0
Quiz 2 (Full: 1 point)
Unknown, LQ is probably 1 though
Quiz 3 (Full: 1 point)
0.98/1/1/1/1/0


2. 20% Midterm
MCs, short questions and long questions. Mostly on concepts. Pseudocode-writing is required for MapReduce. Completely open-book as long as materials are hard-copies.
Stats (Full: 100 points):
67.1/91/76/70/59.5/0

3. 24% HWs (each 8%)
HW1 is about Amazon EC2. Forces you to try it out. Beneficial for project.
HW2 is about MapReduce in Java. Requires Linux VM/Mac/Linux for docker because of Hadoop. Skeleton is given and implementation isn't much.
HW3 is about linear regression with PySpark. Recommended to run on DataBricks. Useful to try out PySpark for final exam
Stats:
HW1 (Full: 8 points)
7.74/8/8/8/7.5/5
HW2 (Full: 8 points)
6.65/8/8/8/6/0
HW3 (Full: 8 points)
7.87/0/8/8/8/8

4. 16% Project
2-4 person project with a self-selected topic. Grading depends on group size. Lots of people did machine-learning-related projects. A report is required.
Stats (Full: 20 points):
16.77/20/18/16.5/15.5/14

5. 30% Final Exam
Very similar to midterm. Large proportion (~40-50%?) is on PySpark programming (handwriting code/output). Completely open-book as long as materials are hard-copies.
Stats (Full: 100 points):
59.88/16/53/61/71/89

Letter grade seems to be quite nice
My assignments and in-class quizzes got full; Project below LQ; Midterm < median a tiny bit; Final > UQ a tiny bit; This yields a B+.

comment_content:
The course will cover the following topics.
(1) Basic concepts of Cloud Computing and production Cloud services;
(2) MapReduce - the de facto datacenter-scale programming abstraction - and its open source implementation of Hadoop.
(3) Apache Spark - a new generation parallel processing framework - and its infrastructure, programming model, cluster deployment, tuning and debugging, as well as a number of specialized data processing systems built on top of Spark.
comment_teaching: Great storytelling including history of cloud computing and explaining system design decisions for Hadoop, Spark, MapReduce, etc..
comment_workload:
4 assignments
1 project
1 final

Assignment 1 on using AWS services, particularly running, managing EC2 instance.
Assignment 2 heaviest on using MapReduce for WordCount, Relative Frequencies, both using PairofStrings and Stripes data structure.
Assignments 3 and 4 on Spark can be found from online sources.

Final project is open-ended

comment_teaching: The instructor has good pronunciation so there was not any difficulty to understand what he said; he sometimes explains the things with daily examples, it was really helpful for understanding. However, the quality of lab classes are bad which were taught by TAs. I basically did not understand more than half of the content of some lab classes and even some TAs just show ppt slides without any coding examples.
comment_workload: In this course, you have to figure things out by yourself like many COMP courses. (Use google to find the way to solve). Workload is pretty light compared to other 4000's courses in UST. If you know how to do the assignments, it will be just a piece of cake. Some assignments need coding explaining presentation so you would better to understand what you wrote on the screen.
comment_grading:
-Homework and labs (30%)
Most assignments are based on programming languages such as Java, Python, SQL and etc, but it would not be that harsh if you had taken any basic courses of programming; there's a lot of sample codes on assignment file, so just need some applications of it. The difficulty of assignment is not that bad, is manageable.

-Programming on Amazon EC2 and locally hosted VMs
Open-ended course project (20%)
Dr. WANG provides numerous options for this project and one of them is free topic. You need to form the group before the midterm commences, so you'd better to find your members ASAP. The grading of the project is not that strict, just need some application of what we have learned so far.

-Final exam (50%)
The worst part of this course. The exam was almost 2 hours long but all the questions require long answers with specific reasons and 40% of the exam encloses coding questions which are really time consuming. It would be really helpful if you are able to get past papers to prepare the final. The average of Final exam was 54.18.

comment_content: The course start with same basic topics related to cloud and infrastructure. Then some MapReduce concept and algorithm are taught with use of python2 and spark. Finally, covering some extra topic like serverless programming and future development of cloud service.
comment_teaching: Most of the time the lecturer explain the contents with the bunch of pictures and conceptual diagram. Not much hand-on experience on cloud and serverless programming is taught.
comment_workload:
Adequate.
You need to submit assignment through github classroom.
The level of assignment is easy and you may refer to different sources for reference.

comment_content:
The course gives an introduction to Cloud Computing. You will learn about the various Cloud providers, how to use AWS, Spark, Hadoop, HDFS, MapReduce, etc.

IMHO, the course is not too interesting. But if you absolutely adore Big Data and Cloud, you might enjoy the course.

Many points discussed about in the lecture notes aren't thorough enough, and require more researching online to understand completely.

Python 2 was used. And due to the quick changes in this area, newer features of the tools aren't explained.
comment_teaching:
The accent of the instructor is quite hard to decipher and requires some effort in listening. He pronounces English words like how they "would have sounded on paper", e.g. "cached" as "ke-shed" like in Shakespearean English. He also has a strong Mandarin accent. I have misheard some words from him and didn't notice until some times later.

His thought process isn't quite easy to understand since his sentences don't really follow the: topic => elaboration => conclusion structure. So it's quite hard to grasp some points of what he wanted to say. It's more like a listening exam.
comment_workload:
The assignments are too easy and there isn't any feeling of accomplishment after completing them.

The group project took us some time to complete and there were quite some points we didn't understanding after picking our topic of reimplementing "Serverless functions" in open sourced tools in Kubernetes, OpenFAAS, and etc.
comment_grading:
The grade consists of assignments, a group project and the final exam.

The final take home exam took me ~12hrs to complete.

comment_content:
Big data should be very useful in the future. Content include what is big data and tools analyzing big data.
The focus should be on the tools, including hadoop and spark. Both requires strong programming skills. The tools are difficult to understand especially if you are not from computer science. Unless you are taking the minor for big data, do not take this course.
Project is very free and about any big data system or you create one. Idea and adequate execution are more important than having a super hard project. Also, most group can get full mark so it is not that important after all.
comment_teaching:
Professor tried his best to demonstrate the materials and tools. However, it is quite difficult to understand. Furthermore, the speed is quite fast. Overall, his notes are organized, but you have to focus and jot notes or most of his notes will not be understandable.
Lab are quite useful in understanding lecture materials.
comment_workload:
You will always hear the professor emphasizing on the heavy workload of the course. If you have no knowledge on big data tools, you will have to spend much time understanding the course materials. They are really tough to understand.
But no midterm reduced the workload in the middle of the term.
comment_grading: No midterm, 5 assignments, 1 project and final exam. Scores are given at a relatively loose way so everyone gets high score. However, grade are given very badly. Since all scores are published, median is C+. That means over half of the class get C range. Two failed. It is definitely not a good grade course.

comment_content:
这门课的主要内容:
- Cloud fundamentals (economics, pricing)
- Cloud service (type of cloud, Paas, Iaas, Saas)
- Virtualization
- Google file system, hadoop distributed file system
- Map reduce
- Hadoop
- Spark (internal, programming, RDD, dataframe)
- Graph analytic (parallel BFS, page rank)
- Cluster management & scheduling, delay scheduling

这门课涵盖了很多知识点。虽然一些部分并没有讲得很仔细，但教授都有把最重要的知识(map reduce, hadoop, spark)教得很好。无论是对于未来想走software engineering，data science还是data engineering的学生，都非常推荐上这门课。

正因为这门课教了非常多知识，所以很难一一去深入了解。而且lecture note很多时候给的资料并不够充足，所以要深入了解的话，一定要自己上网多找资料。教授提供了很多recommended readings，建议大家把它们读完，会帮助深入了解课堂的知识。

建议大家大三上这门课，这样就可以在大三暑假找一些data engineering的intern。少部分data science职位也会要求hadoop和spark。
comment_teaching: 教授教书很热情，也会教很多额外的知识（大多数都来自recommended readings）。他reply email非常快，处理事情也很有效率。
comment_workload:
一共要完成五份assignments，第三份assignment让大家实现课堂上教的map reduce算法。最后两份让大家写spark去manipulate RDD，个人感觉是最重要的。

project方面，只要牵涉到cloud computing和big data就可以了，或者machine learning也可以。教授建议使用FYP的project，或者也可以在UROP project上进行一些改动就好。
comment_grading:
final: mean 66，highest 88
本人: assignment全满，final 79，project 26/25 —> A

今年只给了20% A range。如果要得好grade，project一定要做得很好，因为大家在final都考得不错。

comment_content: Big data systems, including Cloud Computing and parallel data processing frameworks, emerge as enabling technologies in managing and mining the massive amount of data across hundreds or even thousands of commodity servers in datacenters. This course exposes students to both the theory and hands-on experience of this new technology. The course will cover the following topics. (1) Basic concepts of Cloud Computing and production Cloud services; (2) MapReduce - the de facto datacenter-scale programming abstraction - and its open source implementation of Hadoop. (3) Apache Spark - a new generation parallel processing framework - and its infrastructure, programming model, cluster deployment, tuning and debugging, as well as a number of specialized data processing systems built on top of Spark. By walking through a number of hands-on labs and assignments, students are expected to gain first-hand experience programming on real world clusters in production datacenters.
comment_teaching:
The professor is a mainland guy, U of Toronto grad. I think this is his first time teaching a UG course.

Overall great guy: great teaching, explains various concepts really well, responds to emails real fast, and MEMES in lecture notes (I am not joking).

The only downside I think is his accent which is a little bit difficult for me to comprehend.
comment_workload:
For a level 4 CS course,  the workload of this course is okay-ish

Workload:
- 5 programming assignments
- Labs (not every week)
- 1 course project
- 1 Final exam

Homework and lab: full mark, course project: 24/25, final exam: 35.78 / 45 (avg: 27.3)

Overall: good grade, 25% of the class got A range
